ARTICLES.title: Articles (a, an, the)
ARTICLES.description: Articles are used before nouns. 'A/an' for non-specific items, 'the' for specific items. Zero article is used for uncountable nouns and generalizations.
rules: 'A' before consonant sounds: a cat, a university, a European
'An' before vowel sounds: an apple, an hour, an MBA
'The' for specific/known items: the book we discussed
No article for general plurals and uncountable nouns: Dogs are loyal. Water is essential.
'The' with superlatives: the best, the most important
'A/an' with professions: She is a doctor
examples: ✅ I saw a dog. The dog was brown. (first mention = a, second = the)
❌ I saw dog in park.
✅ She is an engineer.
❌ She is engineer.
✅ The sun rises in the east.
❌ I am student. → ✅ I am a student.
common_mistakes: Missing article: 'I am student' → 'I am a student'
Wrong article: 'a hour' → 'an hour'
Unnecessary article: 'the life is beautiful' → 'life is beautiful' (general statement)
Missing 'the' with specific reference: 'Give me book' → 'Give me the book'

CLARITY.title: Clarity & Awkward Phrasing
CLARITY.description: Making sentences clear, concise, and easy to understand. Avoid ambiguity, redundancy, and unnecessarily complex structures.
rules: Avoid ambiguous pronouns — make it clear what 'it', 'this', 'that' refer to
Keep related words together in a sentence
Use active voice when possible for directness
Break complex sentences into simpler ones
Remove redundant words and filler phrases
examples: ❌ The meeting is scheduled for Monday which is important.
✅ The important meeting is scheduled for Monday.
❌ There are many factors that contribute to success that we need to consider.
✅ We need to consider many factors that contribute to success.
❌ What I want to say is that basically we should start.
✅ We should start.
common_mistakes: Vague pronoun reference: 'They told them that they...' — who is who?
Overly long sentences with too many clauses
Redundant phrases: 'in my opinion, I think...'
Unnecessary hedging: 'It seems like maybe we could possibly...'

PREPOSITIONS.title: Prepositions
PREPOSITIONS.description: Words that show relationships between nouns/pronouns and other words in terms of time, place, direction, and more.
rules: Time: 'at' for exact times, 'on' for days/dates, 'in' for months/years/longer periods
Place: 'at' for specific points, 'on' for surfaces, 'in' for enclosed spaces
Direction: 'to' for destination, 'toward' for direction, 'from' for origin
Dependent prepositions: some verbs/adjectives require specific prepositions
examples: ✅ I arrive at 6pm.
❌ I arrive to 6pm.
✅ The book is on the table.
✅ We live in New York.
❌ She is good in math. → ✅ She is good at math.
❌ I'm interested for this. → ✅ I'm interested in this.
common_mistakes: 'arrive to' → 'arrive at/in'
'different than' → 'different from'
'depend of' → 'depend on'
'interested for' → 'interested in'
'good in' → 'good at'

SENTENCE_STRUCTURE.title: Sentence Structure
SENTENCE_STRUCTURE.description: Correct word order and sentence construction in English. English follows Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) order with specific rules for questions, negations, and complex sentences.
rules: Basic order: Subject + Verb + Object (I eat apples)
Questions: Auxiliary + Subject + Verb (Do you eat apples?)
Negation: Subject + Auxiliary + not + Verb (I do not eat apples)
Subordinate clauses can come before or after the main clause
Avoid sentence fragments (incomplete sentences missing subject or verb)
examples: ❌ Is very important to study. → ✅ It is very important to study.
❌ Because I was tired. (fragment) → ✅ I left because I was tired.
❌ The report that we discussed yesterday and that was very detailed. (no main verb)
✅ The report that we discussed yesterday was very detailed.
❌ I don't know what is this. → ✅ I don't know what this is.
common_mistakes: Missing subject: 'Is important' → 'It is important'
Sentence fragments: subordinate clauses used as complete sentences
Wrong question word order in embedded questions: 'what is this' → 'what this is'
Double negation: 'I don't know nothing' → 'I don't know anything'

SUBJECT_VERB_AGREEMENT.title: Subject-Verb Agreement
SUBJECT_VERB_AGREEMENT.description: Verbs must agree with their subjects in number (singular/plural) and person (first/second/third).
rules: Singular subject + singular verb: He runs, She works
Plural subject + plural verb: They run, We work
Collective nouns (team, family, group) usually take singular verbs
'Everyone', 'each', 'nobody' — always singular
Subjects joined by 'and' take plural verb; 'or'/'nor' — verb matches nearest subject
examples: ✅ The cat runs fast.
✅ The cats run fast.
❌ Everyone are here. → ✅ Everyone is here.
❌ She don't know. → ✅ She doesn't know.
❌ There is many people. → ✅ There are many people.
❌ The team are winning. → ✅ The team is winning.
common_mistakes: 'They was' → 'They were'
'She don't' → 'She doesn't'
'There is many' → 'There are many'
'Everyone are' → 'Everyone is'
'The news are' → 'The news is' (uncountable noun)

TENSE_CONSISTENCY.title: Tense Consistency
TENSE_CONSISTENCY.description: Maintaining the same verb tense throughout a sentence or paragraph unless there is a clear reason to shift. Mixing tenses without justification confuses the reader.
rules: Keep the same tense within a sentence unless the time frame changes
Narrative: choose past or present and stick with it
Use past perfect for events before another past event: 'I had finished before she arrived'
Conditional sentences follow specific tense patterns (if + past → would + base)
Reported speech shifts tense back: 'He said he was tired'
examples: ❌ She walked to the store and buys milk. (past + present)
✅ She walked to the store and bought milk.
❌ I was working when he comes in.
✅ I was working when he came in.
❌ If I will have time, I will go. → ✅ If I have time, I will go.
❌ He said he is tired. → ✅ He said he was tired.
common_mistakes: Mixing past and present in the same sentence without reason
Using 'will' in if-clauses: 'If I will go' → 'If I go'
Forgetting to shift tense in reported speech
Inconsistent tense in lists: 'She likes reading, swam, and to cook'

VERB_FORM.title: Verb Forms
VERB_FORM.description: Using the correct form of verbs: infinitive, gerund, past participle, and conjugated forms. Includes irregular verbs and verb patterns.
rules: After modal verbs (can, should, must, will) use base form: 'She can swim'
After 'to' use base form (infinitive): 'I want to go'
Some verbs require gerund (-ing): enjoy, avoid, consider, suggest
Some verbs require infinitive (to + verb): want, need, decide, plan
Irregular past forms must be memorized: go → went, see → saw, choose → chose
examples: ❌ She can to swim. → ✅ She can swim.
❌ I enjoy to read. → ✅ I enjoy reading.
❌ They have went. → ✅ They have gone.
❌ He suggested to go. → ✅ He suggested going.
❌ I have chose. → ✅ I have chosen.
❌ She maked a cake. → ✅ She made a cake.
common_mistakes: Using 'to' after modals: 'can to go' → 'can go'
Wrong verb pattern: 'enjoy to read' → 'enjoy reading'
Wrong past participle: 'have went' → 'have gone'
Regularizing irregular verbs: 'maked' → 'made', 'goed' → 'went'

WORD_CHOICE.title: Word Choice / Diction
WORD_CHOICE.description: Using the right word for the context. Includes commonly confused words, false friends, collocations, and register appropriateness.
rules: Learn commonly confused pairs: affect/effect, than/then, its/it's
Use correct collocations: 'make a decision' (not 'do a decision')
Match register to context: formal writing vs casual speech
Avoid false friends from your native language
Use precise words rather than vague ones
examples: ❌ We protest $10M per month. → ✅ We process $10M per month.
❌ Your advice effected me. → ✅ Your advice affected me.
❌ I did a mistake. → ✅ I made a mistake.
❌ She said me that... → ✅ She told me that...
❌ I am agree. → ✅ I agree.
common_mistakes: affect (verb) vs effect (noun): 'The change affected/had an effect on results'
than (comparison) vs then (time): 'Better than before, and then we left'
make vs do: 'make a mistake', 'do homework'
say vs tell: 'say something', 'tell someone something'
Wrong collocation: 'do a decision' → 'make a decision'

WORD_ORDER.title: Word Order
WORD_ORDER.description: English has a relatively fixed word order. Incorrect word order can change meaning or make sentences ungrammatical.
rules: Standard order: Subject + Verb + Object (SVO)
Adjective order: opinion → size → age → shape → color → origin → material → purpose
Adverbs of frequency go before the main verb but after 'be': 'I always go' / 'She is always late'
Time expressions usually go at the end: 'I went to school yesterday'
Questions invert subject and auxiliary: 'Are you coming?'
examples: ❌ I like very much this song. → ✅ I like this song very much.
❌ She speaks well English. → ✅ She speaks English well.
❌ I yesterday went to school. → ✅ I went to school yesterday.
❌ A blue big car. → ✅ A big blue car. (size before color)
❌ Always I am late. → ✅ I am always late.
common_mistakes: Placing adverbs between verb and object: 'I like very much it' → 'I like it very much'
Wrong adjective order: 'a red big ball' → 'a big red ball'
Placing time expressions in the wrong position
Putting adverbs of frequency in wrong position relative to 'be' vs other verbs
